transcript.transcript: I joined the IHM Academy Boosters, which I became involved in on my return from the Army, and thoroughly enjoyed working with the young people of the IHM Academy. At that period of time, I was invited to serve on the Park and Recreation Board, And after two years, I was named chairman and remained chairman for six years. And I guess that led me into city politics. It was something I enjoyed and seemed to want to expand into and ran for City Council and served City Council for eight years and then on into mayor. That's good, Don. Don, I'm not a native of Coeur d'Alene, but I've been here since 1970, and I think that you're one of the first people that I had met. I was kind of active in the athletic end of the quarter eight high school and of course mid-school and attended the old quarterback club. That's where we met, right. Maybe you could give the people just a brief resume here of why you got active in athletics so to speak. I remember your daughter, I'm trying to think of her name right now and I guess it's My age is showing, but I really... Well, it had to be Tammy, Tina, Jody. The really good, they were all good athletes. And it's kind of unusual for a guy to get so involved in athletics when he doesn't have too many sons that participated. Well, I had three sons also. Oh, okay. And they were mostly into football because that's all really we had at IHM. A limited amount of basketball when we went through, but when I came back from the service, why that was part of our charge was to help our athletic endeavors at IHM expand and grow. And I ran into people like Jack Hartnett, Bill McFarland, who were active in that and encouraged me to get into that. And it just expanded from there. It was really fun working with the kids. And my kids really got into athletics after, with the exception of Terry, who finished out at IHM. About that time the IHM closed and they got into the high school program where there were more opportunities available and especially with the girls because at IHM there were no sports activities for the girls. So when they got into sports, I was, I think, still playing a little bit of softball, And I helped them, the girls especially, develop in the softball area. And a few of them went on into basketball. And I certainly encouraged their participation. And they enjoyed it. And some of them got pretty good with the baseball. Don, one of the things I wanted to know, you failed to mention here, and I've met and I know your lovely wife. Was your wife a local girl or did you— I met her at college. She's a Lewiston native. Her folks were from the Kindred, North Dakota area. They came out here in Pioneers in a, excuse me, Model T Ford on the trails, no highways at that time, and settled in Lewiston, and that's where she was born, and I met her at college. God was good to me. I met your wife, and I think he was good to you. Don, you talked about being involved, actively involved in the IHM Academy, and through that association kind of led you into city politics. In a nutshell, basically, what was your reason for running for the City Council? I was being encouraged and I guess pushed by Bill McFarland. The McFarland family at that time was actively involved in city politics. The McFarland, Bill and his father and his father before that were all city attorneys. And we both served on the IHM board, and Bill encouraged me to get involved in city politics more than anybody. My election to the Parks and Recreation Commission got me closer to city involvement and I guess with the schooling I had it was enjoyable. I enjoyed helping other people, seeing programs put together, and seeing them come to a conclusion. And I enjoyed it. It was a lot of fun. Flag football, I believe, started when I was on the Parks and Recreation Commission and then later involved into tackle football. I'm not going to take credit for each one, but during my term on the commission, these two programs really came into their strength. Was Red Halpert already involved in the Parks and Recreation? Yes, Red was chairman of the Parks Commission when I was, or superintendent, for lack of a better word, of the Parks and Recreation Commission. I worked with Red from the first day. Vander Hill. Don, in going through your resume here, you were elected to the City Council in 1970. I believe that was the year that John McHugh was elected mayor of the city. Being around Corton Lane for a few years, as I have, I get the distinct feeling that you and John are very close friends and... Yes we are, Denny. He lives across the street and down a couple houses and the family has been close. John encouraged me to run. Yes, we've been very close and he's been very influential in my life and my interest in politics because John is a very involved person and very community-minded, and I follow a lot of his leads. This is probably maybe asking quite a bit, but being elected into the city council the first time in 1970, and then obviously the people wanted you back and you were re-elected for another term. I'm wondering if maybe you could hit on some highlights that maybe stick out in your mind back in the 70s when you were on the Council. Some of the issues that you and your fellow Council members had to deal with, and I realize that maybe because the city is growing and things are a little more complex now, to us now it would have seemed not as difficult to run the city back in those days, but I'm sure it was just as difficult, it was just on a smaller scale. So if there are some issues that, I don't mean anything controversial, just some things that you had to deal with, maybe you could give us a little hint on what went on. Well, John was a good mayor. He was a hands-on type mayor. At that time, we didn't have a city administrator who more or less ran the shop, as the city administrators do in today's government. John was actively involved in keeping the ship on a good course. And I learned a lot through him. And I guess the most notable accomplishment in those first four years was our efforts at enlarging the sewer treatment plant and the related problems that go along with it. Several bond issues had failed prior to John becoming mayor and it was becoming very critical. The federal government was putting more and more restrictions on the quality of water. And John just took the bull by the horns and charged ahead, formed some citizens committee, went from house to house, so to speak, from meeting to meeting, and got the people involved and tried to get the bond issue passed. And he was successful at it. The next bond issue, which was for, I forget the total amount, but at that time it seemed astronomical. And it was passed on revenue bonds. John got it passed over to 80%. And we learned a lot of lessons at that time, because I think the major lesson I learned is that if you want the people to get behind him, you have to keep them informed. And that was a good lesson, and John was a good teacher in that respect. During that period of time, government was growing to the point where it was really becoming hard to manage by the form we had, the hands-on mayor type deal. So we helped, I helped John put together the first ordinance which created the job of city administrator. And under John's term, he hired the first city administrator. Unfortunately, we wandered through about six of them before we found somebody that would fit into the mold. Let me interject. Was the first one you hired Dusty? No. No. Okay. Dusty came later on. Okay. I was trying to think of that, but I was naming it out. The first one we hired, the name escapes me, but he didn't last very long. And we wandered through several of them. Because this was a new concept in early in, well, it was a new concept in Idaho. We had city manager form, but this was different than city manager. The city administrator ordinance that we created was totally responsible to the mayor and council, where city manager by law has things that the council or mayor can't can't touch with in certain areas. So we we were starting with a new concept and and finding that square peg to put in the square hole was somewhat difficult. But we learned a lot along the way and we struggled along and I think right now the system is working pretty good. Okay, it seems though I'm going to ask you a couple other questions here along with this. It seems as though in following the papers in the past few years there's always a specific group that has something that seems to take up a lot of the council members' time, and maybe not rightfully so. I'm wondering, I want to just give a couple of examples that I remember and then maybe if you could mention or think back and recall back in the early 70s if there was something that people were kind of opposed that the Council felt we needed to do to move ahead and eventually did. I'm thinking of, it seemed like the city council here a few years ago had to spend a great deal of time with the dog ordinance issue here. A leash law and one of our council members, I think, became a council member, she had a problem with the dog. And that took up a lot of their time. And even though it might have been good in the press and was kind of humorous, I'm sure six people who are elected and paid damn little had to spend a lot of time, you know, with something that didn't bother, shouldn't have bothered the city. The biggest issue before any government is dogs. I don't know why it is, but there are some people and consider their dogs more than a best friend, I think in some cases they would put them above their children. That's difficult for me to understand, but because of that care and concern they have for their pets, and in their own mind, rightfully so, it does get you in uncompromising positions at times, because the example I used was rather humorous at the time. This particular individual had complained and complained, and to his mind, was not getting satisfaction from government, to the fact that he was working in the evenings, and He would come home and try and sleep during the day, and of course dogs were running loose and barking and disturbing his peace. So he got the bright idea one day to set a tape recorder outside of his house and to tape the barking dogs through the day. Well, his answer was to come home at 3 o'clock in the morning and plug in the tape recorder, put it on a loudspeaker, and play it back to his neighbors. I know. Without much more comment on that, why, Of course, the police were there within five minutes. And this story got before the council, and we were brought into the middle of this. And it was rather humorous at the time. And it just about came to open warfare. But fortunately, cooler heads prevail, and he agreed to put away his loudspeaker and the people agreed to keep their dogs from barking. That's great. But it was rather humorous at the time because he played us the recording of the barking dogs and it was it was unbelievable. Don I want to ask you a question, it's been in the before the city for the past seven or eight years, the group of people that whether they're right or wrong, I'm not commenting on that, I'm not asking you to comment whether you felt they were right or wrong, it's kind of the Save Our Shoreline type people that don't want anything to obscure the view of the lake or the mountain. Did you have the same kind of problem back in the early 70s? When I was mayor there was, I had to participate in some of the Save Our Shorelines Council and council meetings and planning commission meetings and and this may surprise you but I think that's healthy government. It helps to get the true issues out on the floor where they're debated and and hopefully it causes more people to participate where you get. The more people you get, the better government you get. And there were some refinements of ordinances. There were some changes in ordinances that weren't agreeable to everybody, but at the same time, I view it as a healthy government, because that's what it's all about, when you get everybody in the room to hammer out their particular problems and come to a common consensus. And while I didn't get into the real heavy Save Our Shorelines during the building of the resort, I'll get a lot of the preliminary concerns on the effort that was made to put up a hotel prior to that. And sure, it's troublesome and makes for very heavy and long meetings. But it's part of the process. And it's really, to me, very satisfying to be able to sit in a room, go through all this, and then come up with a common solution that everybody agrees upon. That's what, to me, the system's all about. What you're saying is you feel this bother than any type of government, specifically small government, If we have total open communication with the people, up front with them, let them speak, work it out, that's great really to solve a lot of problems. The more involvement you get from the people, the better your government's going to be. I think in that respect I like to think that the people have a right to be wrong. If you get enough people involved and it turns out to be an incorrect decision at the time, I have confidence in the people. I think in time, if that decision was wrong at the time, and though it may have been emotional and then the rest of it, I think in time if it were a wrong decision, the people will change it and they will make it right. Because I firmly believe that majority rules and the majority of opinions are by and large the best decisions. I wanted to ask you, and my memory is not serving me as well as it should be, were you on the city council when we had the problem with the firemen? Yes, I was on the city council. Do you think that at that time we had open communication and was there just a... I never was clear on that. I think that was our biggest problem is we did not have good communication. There were some maneuvers by some people that were made in totally good sense in efforts to resolve the problem. But it wasn't totally open. And unfortunately, I don't believe the council was totally informed at at that time I don't mean that as a criticism it's just the way it all evolved because things got a little bit out of control. It's a difficult thing even today to wonder how and it's difficult to and I'm not trying to pin or point a finger at anybody, but it was lack of communication that let it get to as far as it did. And in looking back over, I think I'd have done things differently. I don't know if they'd come out any differently, but I'd have sure tried to have a better line of communication. But sometimes, especially when people start shouting at each other, well, you lose common sense. It seems though, I think a big question that I recall when this was going on was, if this goes down, where does our city stand as far as protection? And it seems as though, I don't remember if I read this or if someone told me that there was a plan put into effect that the city was virtually covered, not as professionally as it was. There were other departments that came in and offered to step in if the firemen went on strike. And it was nothing we planned or had secret meetings about. The department heads came in on their own because their employees had said, tell us when and where and we'll be there. Now, I don't know what level of service you'd have had. You certainly wouldn't have had trained men. But you'd have had people that know how to drive trucks and know how to man hoses. And you could have gone with the best. I really don't believe that it ended up at that harsh a position had we had better lines of communication. But it was an unfortunate thing that happened. I think the courts, and I don't mean this critically, there again it was lack of communication and lack of proper information that got to the courts. I think the courts ended up with some bad decisions that compounded the problem instead of resolving them. And the position got further galvanized, and then it was out of hand. Let me ask you this question. Did it take a very long time to heal those wounds between the city government per se and the fire department? I don't know. I perceive there are a lot of people that still carry a grudge or carry something. I didn't. It didn't bother me that much. I like to feel that I recognized the position the firemen were coming from. I like to feel I also was aware of the position that the elected official was in in trying to protect the city and to make the budget fit into the level of services that were involved. And I personally don't have any hard feelings. You know, I like to think I recognized where each party was coming from. At that time, did we have a city administrator at that time? Yes, we did. I was going to ask you that question earlier on. That was Dusty. When did we get our... Well, first the city administrator came in the first term as council, was that? No, it came during John McHugh's time because that's when we put the ordinance in. And he really, I think my memory is a little shakier in the respect that I think we may have gone through several while he was here, but I think the ordinance was probably only in effect for the last year of his service. So it was after John that really the trial and error became with our—and Ron had a shot at a couple of them. And I—well, Ron hired Dusty, and then I got Dusty when I became mayor, and then Dusty left shortly after I became mayor. Do you recall, Don, in those early 70s, maybe two or three things that you and your fellow council members felt really good about doing in the city, something that you thought really was helpful to the city. I'm wording this improperly. I'm sure that you all felt something there. I think, as an example, Independence Point came about during Ron's service as mayor while I was on the council. And I felt really good in being a part of that. The thing was, I think, brought about by a winter storm that we had that destroyed the old playland pier and in effect destroyed the buildings that were used for dressing rooms and what have you and really did a lot of damage to the foundation. And during Ron's period of time, this came to fruitation in the respect that a lot of the groundwork has done. And fortunately, with a lot of involvement by both council and the mayor, we got together and we ended up with what we call Independence Point. I think it's a great thing for the community. It's a place where the position of day goes by, in winter included, you can now find people at least walking through the area and especially in the summer, you get places wall-to-wall people. That's great. I need, You've probably heard this before, I'm sure you have. I did an interview some time ago with former Mayor Ettinger, and he said that on a few occasions he and I believe his city administrator were accused of torching the old plant there so that they could get rid of that baby. I think they're during the time that the the Great Northern Depot I believe was burned. There were some real suspicious natures as to how the fire started. I certainly don't think any of us or the mayor had any hand in it. But we all looked at it as, boy, that was an easy way to get out of something. I'm sure none of us had any hand in it. I certainly didn't. Well, I know when I first came to Coeur d'Alene, which is in 1970, I don't recall now how long Plainland Pier lasted after that, but it certainly was an eyesore to this community. In its heyday, it was really useful because we had swings, we had a ferris wheel, a carousel, and lots of games for the kids. It was a really fun thing for the kids on weekends, but through the years it deteriorated. The original man who ran the thing, his name escapes me right now, was very instrumental in holding it together and keeping it on a high plane. In his later years He was really out of touch with it, his failing health and what have you. It just deteriorated with his involvement. And it really became what you saw as an eyesore and really a kind of a public nuisance problem. Okay, Don, I want to thank you. This concludes the first half of our tape and if you'll just bear with us a few moments, we'll switch this tape over and continue on the second time on the second side of the tape. You

story.title: Johnston_Donald_OH1657_sideA
story.description: Interviewed by Denny Bozarth on April 12, 1990.
